data.title: Burlington: Stable Growth, Pick Your Spots
data.description:
Burlington Stores, Inc. (BURL) is a retailer that is over 50 years old now. It was, of course, founded in 1972 as the Burlington Coat Factory in New Jersey. Over time it has grown from a small player to regional outerwear wholesaler and now into a major national off-price retailer. The name stems from its opening location and headquarters in Burlington, New Jersey. Today the company operates over 1,000 stores across the United States and Puerto Rico.

It is now just Burlington, as the name was simplified because it has more than just coats and such. It has a big diversified product assortment. It does compete with other off-price retailers, and has had its share of struggles, but shoppers can get up to 60% off compared to traditional retailers. Like competitors the company does opportunistic buying of overstocks and closeouts from manufacturers.

As we look forward, shareholders have a long-term investment case here because the company continues to expand its footprint with plans to reach 2,000 locations. Shares have had a big rally from the fall of 2025 which was a buying opportunity for traders at our investing group, but for now we have to rate it a Hold at these levels.

That said, long term, there is upside, but a better price can likely be had this year. Still, it’s a positive Hold, and the just-reported earnings justify hanging on for longer-term upside. Let us discuss.

Burlington Q4 Sales And Margins

First, we have to check for trends in sales. That is key. In Q4, which covers the holiday sales period mind you we saw revenues up 11.3% to $3.64 billion. This was a slight beat of $50 million versus consensus, but stable and respectable growth billion. Of course for retailers the most important metric we monitor is always comparable sales. Well, comparable store sales were up 4% from a year ago. For all of 2025 the comps were still positive at 2%. The company had taken action to mitigate tariffs in H1 2025, and that translated in H2 2025.

Now, we love to see higher sales and positive comps. But we also like to see expanding gross profit margin, or at the very least, flat margins. Gross profit margins on a percentage basis rose 80 basis points to 43.7%, from 42.9% for the Q4 compared to last year. We view this as a very positive result. Both freight costs and merchandise margins improved, by 20 basis points and 80 basis points, respectively. So, higher sales, better margins.

The next thing to look for in retail is selling and admin expenses. In total dollars these were up to $1.05 billion vs. $0.965 million last year. But here is the key. As a percentage of sales the selling and admin expenses improved 40 basis points to 22.2% compared to 22.6% last year. Accounting for the commensurate rise, but well-controlled operating expenses, adjusted net income was $320 million, or $4.99 per share, vs. $267 million, or $4.14 per share. Further the adjusted EBITDA was $562 million, compared to $456 million. All of this implied consumers are responding well to the discounted nature of Burlington's offerings, and driving earnings higher, which is why shares have rallied.

Burlington Inventory Levels And Balance Sheet

In terms of inventory the company bought a lot of overstock to build up for future promotions and sales, so despite the great sales merchandise inventories were up to $1.31 billion to end the year vs. $1.25 million coming into the year a 5% increase. At the store level inventories increased 12%. Reserve inventory, the items that are going to be sent to stores in the coming months, was 40% of total inventory to end the year, down slightly from 46% to start the year. How about the balance sheet?

We like what we are seeing on the balance sheet front too. Liquidity is robust, as is the cash position. To end 2025, Burlington had $2.16 billion in liquidity. Making up the liquidity is $1.23 billion in cash and $926 million in available credit on its revolving ABL facility. Given the inventory levels, there is not a need to spend this cash, but the company has a ton of flexibility with this liquidity should opportunity arise. As the company is embarking to expand, there is a manageable $2.08 billion in total debt, including $1.72 billion in its term loan facility, but no borrowings on the ABL facility. This is not a concerning leverage given the cash and cash flow.

Further, the company is repurchasing shares. In Q4, the company repurchased 223,863 shares for $59 million. More repurchases are in store as there is $385 million remaining on its share repurchase program authorization through May 2027.

Looking Ahead For Burlington

We are long-term bullish, but after the run we think a Hold rating is appropriate at this valuation, even with the respectable growth outlook. Tariffs are still a concern, the company wants to open more stores, and the economy while still robust is on watch. But a good year of sales growth is expected, as for fiscal 2026 management projects revenues increasing 8% to 10%. Further, it is guiding for 1-3% comparable sales.

So why is the actionable suggestion right now to hold, even though we see longer-term upside? Simply market dynamics. We think it best to wait for some market weakness for new money to enter at this valuation. Certainly the expectation from management for $10.95 to $11.45 in EPS for the year is positive. This would be $11.20 at the midpoint, and this is 10.1% growth at the midpoint. However, at $322 at the time of this writing, shares are at a premium for this growth at nearly 29X FWD EPS. Thus, we think a Hold is justified, with new money best served holding out for sub-$300, closer to 25X, if not cheaper.

data.title: Voya Global Advantage And Premium Opportunity Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Strategy overview

The Fund combines an actively-managed quantitative equity investment strategy with a call writing option strategy, seeking to create a diversified portfolio with enhanced total return potential and strong downside capture over a full market cycle.

Key takeaways

  • During the quarter, the equity sleeve underperformed the reference Index primarily due to the lower beta positioning and higher dividend yield. Conversely, the core model, particularly the sentiment pillar contributed the most.
  • Global conditions in 4Q25 shifted toward monetary easing as inflation moderated and labor markets softened, prompting the U.S. Federal Reserve to cut rates. Tariff pressures persisted, but cautious consumer spending helped prevent inflation from sticking. U.S. gross domestic product (GDP) growth slowed yet remained positive, favoring large-cap equities supported by strong fundamentals and artificial intelligence (AI)-driven productivity.
  • Looking ahead to early 2026, global equities remain uneven. Europe and the United Kingdom face fiscal and structural challenges, while Japan benefits from reforms. China struggles with property stress and weak consumer confidence, though tech-linked offshore equities offer opportunities. Emerging markets (EM) are mixed, and international bonds remain less attractive.

Portfolio review

For the quarter, the Voya Global Advantage and Premium Opportunity Fund provided a total return of 1.07% on a net asset value basis and a total return of 0.57% on a market price basis. For the same period, the Fund's reference Index, the MSCI World Value Index, returned 3.34%

During the quarter, the Fund's equity sleeve underperformed the Index. The largest detractors were the lower beta positioning and higher dividend yield, while the core model contributed.

On the regional level, stock selection in United Kingdom, Japan, and Europe – Euro regions detracted., and selection in the Europe – Non-Euro contributed the most.

On the sector level, stock selection in the industrials, information technology, and consumer staples sectors detracted. Among the key detractors were not owning Micron Technology Inc., and overweight in Paycom Software Inc. and Motorola Solutions Inc.

By contrast, stock selection in the communication services and consumer discretionary sectors contributed the most. Among the key individual stock contributors were not owning Oracle Corp. and the underweight positions in Alphabet Inc. Class A and Meta Platforms Inc. Class A.

Option portfolio

During the quarter, the Fund's option strategy had a positive impact on returns. The strategy seeks to generate premiums and retain some potential for upside appreciation. The Fund implements this strategy by typically writing call options on regional indices, the selection and allocation of which resulted from an optimization intended to track the reference Index of the

Fund closely. The strike prices of the options written were typically out of the money or near the money with expiration dates around six weeks at inception.

data.title: Flywire Corporation: Visible Path For Durable Adj. EBITDA Growth
data.description:
Investment Summary

My previous investment thought on Flywire Corporation (FLYW) was a buy rating because all the growth metrics were healthy, and I believed there were tangible growth catalysts previously. Fast forward ~18 months later, growth is still solid, and FLYW is pushing deeper into workflows through SFS. Margins were messy this quarter, but EBITDA still grew strongly, which supports the operating leverage angle.

4Q25 Results Update

FLYW continues to show growth strength. In this Q4 quarter, reported revenue was up 34% y/y to $157.5 million, albeit Sertifi added $14.2 million, or 1200 bps of growth. Without that, organic growth was still >20%, which is very healthy. Viewed from a revenue-less ancillary services basis (revenue excluding pass-through printing/mailing and marketing fees), revenue was $152.7 million, which was up 35.3% y/y. Operating metrics also saw good trends: total payment volume [TPV] was up ~36% y/y to $9.3 billion, and 31% when excluding Sertifi. The part that made this quarter not as clean was margins. GAAP gross margin contracted close to 600 bps y/y (now at 57.6%), and adj. gross margin contracted by close to 470 bps y/y. Management did say the contraction was due to the mix and ramping up of certain payment processing programs, plus currency settlement impacts. That said, adj. EBITDA still grew strongly by ~52% y/y. Which means adj. EBITDA could’ve been stronger if not for these headwinds.

The Growth Profile Becomes More Durable, and the Business Gets Stickier

While growth is not as strong as I thought previously, I think what matters today is that FLYW showed the market it can grow without needing a perfect education admission backdrop. And understanding how FLYW does it instills confidence that this growth is durable. It is essentially what FLYW calls "Enterprise Transformation via SFS," which is FLYW’s effort to embed itself into how schools run billing and collections, using Student Financial Software [SFS] as the pivot. This changes the growth dynamic entirely because growth is no longer tied to the number of student admissions but rather to being part of a workflow. That makes FLYW a lot harder to displace, and it should make growth less dependent on intake season. And the data points supporting this are pretty direct. Management noted >35% growth in 2025 projected annual recurring revenue [ARR] from new signings (excluding healthcare payment processing contracts) and also a 35% y/y increase in new sales pipeline generation. They also said 80% of education revenue and 70% of travel revenue now come from enterprise clients (those with more than $100,000 of LTM revenue).

This workflow push also shows up in how FLYW is building the “plugs” around SFS and payments. This seems to be an area that some investors may have underestimated. Specifically, FLYW noted they are “Workday Certified” for both Global Payments and SFS and also have an Oracle Fusion integration aimed at UK education. They have also expanded payment rails, including stablecoin capabilities and an expanded TenPay partnership that integrates WeChat Pay for Chinese students paying in South Korea and Malaysia. The reason these efforts are important, in my view, is simple. It doesn’t make sense for schools to adopt another point solution that creates extra steps for their finance teams, and students would find it a hassle (and less trustworthy) when they’re trying to make payment. If FLYW can be plugged into systems these schools already run on and support the payment methods students actually use, I believe it is logical to assume schools will adopt FLYW. The implication is it makes winning deals easier, and it increases the odds that once FLYW is in, it doesn’t get replaced.

Valuation

One last positive aspect to note is operating leverage. As noted in the earnings review section, adj. gross margin fell y/y, but adj. EBITDA still went up by more than 50% to $25.4 million (a 16.6% margin), and GAAP net income went to basically break-even from a loss last year. By my math, FLYW added ~$9 million adj. EBITDA on ~$40 million more revenue (vs. Q4 2024), which means an incremental margin of 22.5%. That is the baseline to think about how much more margin can expand. If we extrapolate this forward and believe management's mid-teens revenue growth guide (assuming no acceleration), I can bridge to this business printing ~20% adj. EBITDA growth CAGR through FY28. A business of this growth profile shouldn’t trade at 7x forward EBITDA if a peer like Bill Holdings (BILL) trades at 12x with mid-teens revenue growth. I think FLYW should at least trade closer to 12x forward EBITDA (now trades at ~7x forward EBITDA vs. ~18x in Sep'24).

Risk

My biggest worry is the market view of FLYW’s profitability. The decline in adj. gross margin is huge, and while I focus on adj. EBITDA growth: if more growth comes from lower-margin payment flows (including the mix shifts management noted), margin could compress further, and the operating leverage angle I mentioned may not play out. That would be a disaster for the stock as both sales and earnings growth decelerate (margin could turn back to negative). Execution of the “Enterprise Transformation via SFS” strategy also carries risk. If integrations and rollouts take longer than expected, it could hurt the pace of growth.

Conclusion

My view for FLYW is a buy rating. The market seems to focus too much on the margin noise and ignores the fact that FLYW can continue to print solid growth and that initiatives being pushed are making the business stickier over time. I also like that operating leverage showed up even with gross margin pressure. The main thing to watch is whether the mix keeps dragging gross margin and caps profit expansion. If that stabilizes, the stock should see a strong re-rating as the market prices in solid adj. EBITDA growth ahead.

data.title: BNY Mellon Small/Mid Cap Growth Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Review

Heading into 2026, the macro backdrop reflects a measured balance.

Equity performance was broadly positive across developed and emerging markets. Cooling inflation across major economies, resilient economic growth and improving visibility on corporate earnings supported investor confidence, despite ongoing geopolitical and policy uncertainty. Risk sentiment was further boosted by selective central bank easing and year-end portfolio rebalancing, underpinning gains across global equities.

The S&P 500® Index increased by 2.66% for the quarter. Within the index, eight of the 11 Global Industry Classification Standard (GICS) equity sectors finished in positive territory, led by health care and communication services. Value stocks outperformed growth stocks. Among other major equity benchmarks, the MSCI EAFE Index, a measure of developed markets excluding the US and Canada, increased by 4.86%, while the MSCI Emerging Markets Index increased by 4.73%.

In the US, equities generally advanced during the fourth quarter. The Dow Jones Industrial Average® increased by 4.03%, while the NASDAQ 100 Index® increased by 2.47%. US fixed-income markets also posted gains. Markets proved resilient amid elevated uncertainty driven by a prolonged federal government shutdown and ongoing geopolitical tensions in the Middle East. Inflation, as measured by the headline consumer-price index, eased gradually from 3.0% year-over-year in September to 2.7% year-over-year in November. Labor market data, while disrupted and delayed, pointed to slowing momentum rather than a sharp deterioration. Economic growth remained firm, with third-quarter gross domestic product (GDP) rising at a solid 4.3% annualized, supported by consumer spending and government outlays despite softer retail sales and business investment. Against this backdrop, the Federal Reserve cut rates by 25 basis points (bps) early in the quarter, while maintaining a cautious tone and reaffirming a data-dependent policy stance as markets looked ahead to 2026.

Equity markets in developed economies were broadly higher during the quarter. In the eurozone, fourth-quarter data signaled easing inflation and mixed growth dynamics. Inflation moderated toward the European Central Bank’s (ECB’s) 2% target, while business activity improved and GDP grew modestly, enabling the ECB to keep rates unchanged throughout the quarter. UK inflation declined steadily from elevated levels, while growth weakened and labor markets softened. The Bank of England shifted from holding rates earlier in the quarter to a 25-bp cut at its December meeting. In Japan, inflation remained above target and unemployment stayed low, despite a third-quarter GDP contraction and softer activity indicators. As a result, the Bank of Japan ended its hold with a late-quarter 25-bp hike, continuing its normalization effort.

Emerging-market equities were positive for the quarter. In China, growth slowed from earlier in the year, and consumer and property-sector weakness persisted; however, data showed tentative stabilization, as manufacturing and services activity returned to slight expansion, enabling the People’s Bank of China to keep lending rates unchanged throughout the quarter. Brazil’s economy softened into the fourth quarter amid widening external deficits and weaker activity indicators. Nonetheless, the central bank maintained its restrictive 15% policy rate despite easing inflation. India stood out with solid momentum, posting robust GDP growth, rising industrial output and relatively contained inflation, supported by government commitments to greater capital spending and reform. In contrast, Mexico faced weak growth and a third-quarter contraction, prompting successive rate cuts as inflation eased. Argentina bolstered financial stability through a sizeable currency-swap agreement and improving US export prospects.

Commodities were slightly higher, with the Refinitiv/CoreCommodity CRB Index rising 0.35%. During the fourth quarter, precious metals posted solid gains, with gold, silver and platinum supported by safe-haven demand amid geopolitical tensions, a US government shutdown and a weaker US dollar, culminating in a robust year-end rally. In contrast, energy markets were softer. Oil prices trended lower throughout the quarter as ample global supply, rising inventories and slowing demand outweighed geopolitical risks. Natural gas was volatile, rising early in the quarter on colder weather, liquefied natural gas export demand and seasonal heating needs, before ending the year lower as weather-driven strength faded. The US dollar weakened against a basket of major currencies.

Performance Review

The fund (Class A shares at NAV)(DBMAX) outperformed its benchmark, the Russell 2500 Growth Index, during the fourth quarter of 2025. From a sector perspective, health care led relative contributors over the period, followed by information technology. Conversely, industrials and financials were the most significant laggards.

Sector Review

Positive Impacts

Health Care: Health care led relative contributors over the period. Positive stock selection in the health care providers and services and life sciences tools and services segments contributed.

Information Technology: The information technology sector also contributed positively to relative performance. Solid stock selection within the semiconductors and semiconductor equipment segment was a key driver, supported by effective positioning and favorable stock selection within the technology hardware, storage, and peripherals subsector.

Negative Impacts

Industrials: Stock selection in industrials weighed on relative performance, particularly within the aerospace and defense and construction and engineering segments.

Financials: Financials also detracted from relative returns over the quarter, largely due to positioning and stock selection within banks and insurance companies.

Stock Review1

Key Contributors – Q4 25

Guardant Health: Guardant Health (GH) continued to benefit from its upbeat third-quarter earnings release, news that the company had boosted its blood-testing capabilities and analyst upgrades.

Repligen Corporation: We continue to favor Repligen’s (RGEN) exposure to secularly growing bioprocessing capabilities. Its share price was boosted by the release of better-than-expected third-quarter results that revealed company-wide revenue growth.

Cidara Therapeutics: Shares of Cidara Therapeutics (CDTX) surged after the company announced in November that it would be acquired by Merck.

Key Detractors – Q4 25

AeroVironment: AeroVironment designs, develops, produces, delivers, and supports a technologically advanced portfolio of intelligent, multi-domain robotic systems and related services for government agencies and businesses. Shares declined after the company reported disappointing earnings and issued a softer profit outlook.

Inspire Medical Systems: Shares of Inspire Medical Systems fell in the fourth quarter after the company cut its 2025 outlook amid US launch setbacks for the next-generation Inspire V system, while rising competition from GLP‑ 1 weight-loss drugs and shifting patient preference toward drug therapy over surgery further pressured the stock.

Soleno Therapeutics: Shares of Soleno Therapeutics fell in the fourth quarter. A sharp quarter-over-quarter decline in new patient start forms for VYKAT XR and persistent concerns over safety and discontinuation rates overshadowed revenues that exceeded analyst expectations.

1. Performance of individual stock evaluated is based on total effect. Total effect represents the opportunity cost of an investment manager's investment decisions relative to an overall benchmark, which can include holdings that are not held by the portfolio but contribute to relative portfolio performance.

Market Outlook

Heading into 2026, the macro backdrop reflects a measured balance. Inflation continues to moderate, with the US consumer-price index (CPI) up 2.7% year-over-year and core CPI up 2.6% year-over-year in November, while wage growth and consumer spending have cooled without pointing to recession. In December, the Federal Reserve (Fed) lowered its target range to 3.50% – 3.75%, signaling a shift toward easing while reaffirming a data-dependent approach amid mixed committee views. Global growth forecasts hover around 3.2% for 2025 and 3.1% for 2026. Supportive factors include selective fiscal measures in China and a gradual recovery in Europe, while trade policy uncertainty and geopolitical risks remain notable headwinds.

Corporate earnings remain resilient, with S&P 500 earnings per share up nearly 13% in the third quarter and projected to rise 8% in the fourth quarter, underpinned by solid margins and improving breadth beyond mega-cap technology. However, forward price-to-earnings ratios are near 21.8, elevated versus historical averages, suggesting limited capacity for multiple expansion without additional rate relief or better-than-expected earnings. In our view, international equities, particularly developed-market value segments, offer relative attractiveness with lower multiples and potential higher dividend yields, while thematic opportunities in artificial intelligence (AI) infrastructure, electrification and energy transition continue to draw capital.

Looking ahead, equities appear biased toward modest gains rather than outsized rallies. We believe positioning may favor quality growth with durable cash flows, complemented by selective cyclicals linked to tangible AI infrastructure and quality small caps that could benefit from policy normalization. Risks include sticky services inflation, labor market deterioration and renewed supply-chain disruptions, while catalysts, such as January consumer-price inflation, Fed communications and fourth-quarter earnings could shape sentiment. In short, we think the environment supports a disciplined allocation framework that balances the potential upside from policy easing and earnings resilience against valuation constraints and geopolitical volatility.

Small-cap equities may be increasingly attractive in 2026, supported by a convergence of macro, policy and earnings momentum. First, anticipated Fed rate cuts in 2026 could benefit small caps, which tend to be more sensitive to lower borrowing costs. Second, valuations remain discounted; Bank of America projects S&P SmallCap 600 earnings-per-share growth of approximately 19% for 2026, above the broader S&P 500, suggesting favorable entry points. Third, cyclical tailwinds like reshoring, AI-driven capital investment, rising merger-and-acquisition activity and signs of a prospective initial-public-offering rebound may offer additional earnings catalysts. We believe that small-cap leadership will likely extend into 2026 as funds rotate out of overvalued mega caps into this historically overlooked segment. Overall, the combination of improving macroeconomics, lower valuations and accelerating earnings momentum suggests a supportive setup for US small caps, with scope for continued recovery and the potential for relative outperformance.

The 2026 outlook for international markets is supported by attractive valuations, policy accommodation in select regions and strengthening macro data. The European Central Bank’s neutral-to-accommodative stance, coupled with fiscal initiatives that are boosting credit growth and industrial activity, supports consensus expectations for approximately 15% earnings-per-share growth in 2026. Meanwhile, Asia may benefit from robust export demand, AI-driven semiconductor momentum and targeted stimulus in China to stabilize property and manufacturing sectors. Regional growth forecasts range from 3.4% to 4.6%. 1 Both regions may offer compelling opportunities in cyclicals, financials and infrastructure-linked sectors, with potential dividend yields and valuation discounts versus US equities adding support. Together, Europe and Asia offer diversified exposure amid a rotation toward broader leadership and structural growth themes.

Risks

Equities are subject to market, market sector, market liquidity, issuer, and investment style risks, to varying degrees. Small and midsized company stocks tend to be more volatile and less liquid than larger company stocks as these companies are less established and have more volatile earnings histories.

1 Source: CapitalMarkets.net, Asia Market Outlook 2026, December 15, 2025.

Definitions

Q is Quarter. NAV is Net Asset Value. YTD is Year to Date. FDIC is Federal Deposit Insurance Corp. CRB is Commodity Research Bureau. AI is Artificial intelligence, which refers to computer systems that can perform tasks typically requiring human intelligence, such as visual perception, speech recognition, decision making and language translation. Sticky inflation is when prices for certain goods and services, like rent, insurance, and medical care, change very slowly and persist at high levels, even as other economic factors shift.

As of 12/31/25, the fund’s top 10 holdings were Guardant Health 3.65%, Karman Holdings 3.44%, Repligen 3.39%, Baldwin Insurance Group 2.96%, Warby Parker 2.91%, Casey's General Stores 2.91%, SiTime 2.88%, AeroVironment 2.81%, MKS 2.73%, Curtiss-Wright 2.69%.

As of 12/31/25, the companies listed represented, in the aggregate, 11.01% of the fund’s portfolio. Cidara Therapeutics, Merck and Inspire Medical Systems were not portfolio holdings as of 12/31/25. The holdings listed should not be considered recommendations to buy or sell a particular security. Other holdings may not have performed as well as some of those listed herein. Portfolio composition is subject to change at any time.

The Russell 2500 TM Growth Index is a widely accepted, unmanaged index that measures the performance of those Russell 2500 companies — the 2,500 smallest companies in the Russell 3000 Index (which is composed of the 3,000 largest U.S. companies based on total market capitalization) — with higher price-to-book ratios and higher forecasted growth values. The S&P 500® Index is widely regarded as the best single gauge of large-cap U.S. equities. The index includes 500 leading companies and captures approximately 80% coverage of available market capitalization. The Morgan Stanley Capital International Europe, Australasia, Far East (MSCI EAFE®) Index is a free float-adjusted, market capitalization-weighted index that is designed to measure equity performance in developed markets, excluding the United States and Canada. The index consists of select designated MSCI national developed market indices. The MSCI Emerging Markets Index is a free float-adjusted, market capitalization-weighted index that is designed to measure the equity performance in global emerging markets. The index consists of 22 MSCI emerging- market national indices. The Dow Jones Industrial Average, Dow Jones, or simply the Dow, is a stock market index of 30 prominent companies listed on stock exchanges in the United States. The Nasdaq-100 Index is a stock market index made up of equity securities issued by 100 of the largest non-financial companies listed on the Nasdaq stock exchange. The Refinitiv/CoreCommodity CRB Index is a benchmark index that tracks the price movements of a basket of 19 globally traded commodities. It is designed to provide a broad representation of the commodity market, and is used as a benchmark for commodity-related investments. GICS stands for the Global Industry Classification Standard, a system developed by S&P Dow Jones Indices and MSCI to categorize companies into sectors and industries based on their principal business activities. data.title: Gen Z Is Threatening The Alcohol Industry
data.description:
Imagine for a moment a scenario where a billion-dollar market realizes its future consumers will no longer buy their product. This might be happening with the alcohol industry. Generation Z is drinking less and less each day, and sometimes, they don’t even start. I’m a millennial who has this trait of Gen Z, since I don’t drink any alcohol at all, not socially, not anything. I might taste something here and there, but nothing more than a sip.

If for millennials this habit sounds strange, imagine for baby boomers… The point is, when an entire generation stops drinking, the impact is not only on pubs and parties but also on public health, taxes, employment, and, of course, global brands and the stock market.

My view here is that I might actually be a bit bearish on any case that treats alcohol as an ever-growing industry. I, personally, wouldn’t buy and hold any beer stock expecting it to grow just as they did in the past, unless the fundamentals of a specific stock are too good and the valuation is extremely attractive. But then it would be more of a math/numbers game than believing in the product or industry.

I’m not saying alcohol will disappear (I’m not that extreme), but I think this “default” social habit is going to change drastically, and the sector will feel it in their sales volume. Therefore, the alcohol industry might have to adapt to bring relevant innovation instead of just producing the same old beers and relying on generational “bad” habits. If that switch to innovation indeed happens, I don’t think all players will be able to afford this R&D, which will make them eventually disappear.

That also doesn’t mean the industry will collapse. What I see is a re-rating of the expectations the market will put on the beer stocks. However, companies with strong branding and pricing power might be able to change their mix and generate sales from low- or no-alcohol beverages and thus remain resilient and profitable.

All in all, I don’t think this is a short-term fluctuation but a behavioral transition. For the investor, this means more scrutiny when stock picking, possibly aiming at companies that have a clear budget/plan for adaptability and innovation rather than mass production only.

Generations and Their Relation With Alcohol

Society puts generations into different boxes because they normally have similar habits, being molded by the same economic, cultural, and technological environment.

If we start with the baby boomers, those are the ones born after World War II. Which means people who grew up in a much more predictable world, with longer professional careers, higher stability, and a habit of drinking alcohol as if it were their second nature, almost like a social ritual.

After the boomers, we have Generation X, which experienced urbanization, higher work intensity, individualism, and the beginning of general market consumption as a lifestyle. This generation started to adopt the “every weekend, I drink” habit.

After Generation X, we have the millennials. Those were born in the midst of an economic crisis, high inflation in multiple parts of the globe, swift changes in the job market, and a clearly more expensive adult life. This generation started to treat alcohol as a personal choice rather than something that was embedded in human nature. But still, most millennials drink at least socially.

Lastly, we get to Generation Z, the first generation that grew up with the internet in the palm of their hands, being constantly influenced by social media and with real-time information at their disposal. With all that load of information and habits being shown to the world, this generation started to realize that they might not need alcohol to have fun or for it to be present in every part of their lives… or maybe not in any part of it. And that’s where the alcohol logic started to fall apart.

Alcohol Consumption Dropped Worldwide

To begin, research shows that Generation Z consumes about 20% less alcohol than millennials. Looking specifically at wine and beer, the gap is even bigger, reaching something like 33% less than previous generations.

Alcohol was present everywhere, from college and happy hours to weddings and birthdays, not necessarily because everyone wanted to drink, but because it was the social norm. Now, pubs and clubs are already finding it hard to attract the new generation, and when they do, how are they going to monetize the clientele without selling alcoholic beverages?

In the beginning, it might seem like a temporary trend, but I believe it’s not. Businesses in those industries now have to change their marketing strategy considerably, and they have to face the fact that they are going to lose all the billions they spent throughout the decades trying to make alcohol look like an essential part of life... and Gen Z already knows it’s not.

Statistics also show that 70% of Gen Z is choosing non-alcoholic beverages, while 18%-20% of young Americans between 21 and 28 years old also don’t drink beer, wine, or spirits on a regular basis. And this is not just in the US. In the UK, for example, one in four young people are abstinent, according to data from the Health Survey for England, and young people aged between 16 and 24 who don’t drink alcohol range from 25% to 30%.

In Germany, alcohol consumption has also dropped. In 2024, 68% of Germans said they drink alcohol, compared to 78% in 2015, according to data from YouGov. Within the German Gen Z, 61% say they drink, but one in four reduced consumption or stopped completely in the past five years. The result? 52 breweries closed between 2023 and 2024, the biggest decline in three decades. Yes, even Germany is taking a hit from the new healthy generation.

Now, talking about my home, Brazil also followed the trend. In 2025, 64% of Brazilians affirmed they didn’t consume alcohol, against 55% in 2023, according to this data from CISA. But then again, the young folks are the ones changing their habits the most. Abstinence rose from 46% to 64% among people between 18 and 24 years old and from 47% to 61% for folks aged between 25 and 34.

All right, enough data spilling. The point is not whether this trend change exists, because it does. The question is how it's affecting the industry, but this time let's look in more detail.

What's the Impact on the Industry

From what I see, when an entire generation consumes less alcohol, three different components feel the major hit: revenue, inventory, and jobs.

Decline in Sales Volume and Market Value

Anheuser-Busch InBev (BUD), or simply AB InBev, the largest brewer in the world, has already reported a drop in volume. This, in my view, is quite relevant because the company might adjust the price and even the product mix, but volume is linked directly to consumer habits.

In Brazil, volume fell 4.5% in the year and 2.6% in the fourth quarter. In Argentina, it declined 0.9% in the year and 4.9% in the fourth quarter. And in Canada, it fell 1.3% in the year and 0.7% in the quarter. Which means it’s not a local event, as we had already discussed.

Broadly speaking, according to data from IWSR (International Wine and Spirits Record), the total volume for alcoholic beverages dropped 1% in 2024 in major markets, and a new drop of 0.4% is expected for 2025.

The consequence, as mentioned in the beginning, is felt in the financial market as well. The global beverage sector is 46% below its 2021 peak, with a cumulative loss of about $830 billion in market value in just over four years.

High Inventories and Job Cuts

Inventories have also become a major problem, with big and publicly traded spirits companies sitting on a pile of unsold beverages worth about $22 billion. The reason is that this specific market is a big fan of planning things far in advance.

Take the whiskeys, for example, that require more than a decade of aging before being launched in the market. You can’t simply predict with precision that you won’t have consumers decades ahead, so you can’t simply halt your current production.

The outcome? Immobilized capital, storage costs, financial costs, and devaluation risk, which tend to create pressure on margins and a possible price war, as heavy discounts and sales are required.

And finally, this pressure ends up hitting the labor market. Take Heineken N.V. (HEINY), for instance, which announced the cut of 6,000 jobs, about 7% of its global workforce. They explained it was due to a weaker demand and slower expected growth.

The Growth of Non-Alcoholic Beer

As an attempt to adapt, the industry is obviously changing its portfolio, with money migrating from alcoholic beverages to non-alcoholic drinks. In Brazil, for example, non-alcoholic beer consumption jumped from 133 million liters in 2018 to 750 million liters in 2022. On top of that, Euromonitor projects 885 million liters in 2026, almost six times the 2018 volume.

According to IWST, global sales of low- to non-alcoholic drinks reached $11 billion in 2022, a 37.5% increase compared with 2018. This means that the industry was already predicting or experiencing this trend change at some point, repositioning its portfolio. However, the costs for this innovation are high, and not all companies have the capital to invest in R&D of new flavors, marketing strategies, distribution, and product development.

How Investors Can Position for This Trend Change

If what I discussed until now makes sense, especially regarding lower volume growth and pressure on margins and valuation, then there might be some moves investors can make.

Let me take a quick example to better illustrate this dynamic. For instance, Brown-Forman Corporation (BF.B) Jack Daniel’s owner reported ~$1 billion in quarterly revenue (-5.39% YoY) and signaled a decline in organic sales and profits for the next fiscal year. So, with roughly $4 billion in annual sales, even a modest 5% revenue decline represents a relevant amount of about $200 million in lost sales (0.05 × $4 billion). In the event that Brown-Forman manages to cut $70–$80 million in costs, that would cover only 35–40% of the impact, still leaving $120–$130 million in operating pressure.

Therefore, a possible approach would be to reduce exposure to companies highly dependent on alcohol volume, and then allocate part of this exposure to beverage sectors that are more resilient, if you still wish to stay invested in the industry.

An example is the Consumer Staples Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLP), which is around 19% exposed to beverages, with relevant positions in Coca-Cola (KO) (~6.4%), PepsiCo (PEP) (~4.7%), and Monster (MNST) (~3.6%). So, a $10,000 investment in this ETF would represent about $1,900 of indirect exposure to the beverages sector, but with much more diversification.

Another more “targeted” option would be the Invesco Food & Beverage ETF (PBJ), which has a higher allocation to Monster (~5%) and also includes names like Celsius (CELH) (~3.37%)

Lastly, if you really want to stock pick, I would recommend global brewers with a strong emerging market presence and an inclination to scale their no/low-alcohol products. For this scenario, I can only think of Heineken, which is having tremendous success with its Heineken 0.0 and seems to be engaged and willing to innovate.

So, in short, I believe investors who wish exposure to this sector should approach it as less “betting against alcohol” and more “let’s reallocate within the beverages sector.” And they can do that through those ETFs I mentioned or through companies that clearly intend to do something about this major change in alcohol consumption.

Alcohol Consumption Will Not Disappear

I don’t think at all that alcohol consumption will disappear one day, not even in the distant future. It will simply decline drastically, and the market will have to adapt. As for my personal take on this, I might be biased because I don’t drink, but I can’t deny that I see this as something positive; after all, it’s been proven that alcohol, in any form or shape, is bad for your health.

But as for the financial market and what investors can expect, I would definitely think twice before jumping into any stock or ETF from the beer industry with a buy-and-hold approach. From what we discussed, I don’t have much faith in this general market, but that does not mean you won’t find good bargains here and there. Finally, if you plan on finding asymmetries in this market, go for value instead of growth.

data.title: SCHD: When AI Hype Fades And Geopolitics Bite
data.description:
Reasons To Continue Buying SCHD

Schwab US Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD) thrives in the current environment of mounting geopolitical uncertainty and increasing "AI bubble" fears. The ETF has delivered a total return of almost 16% since my previous bullish article in December, which was called "SCHD: What if AI Capex Returns Disappoint?." The Q4 earnings season revealed that investors are indeed becoming increasingly concerned about the ability of tech giants to maintain their historically strong returns after massive increases in capital spending.

Most of the Magnificent 7 companies delivered impressive Q4 financial results, but none of them demonstrate strong share price performance in 2026 so far, with only Meta (META) delivering a modest positive YTD stock performance. The fact that big tech companies demonstrate weak share price momentum despite impressive earnings signals deteriorating sentiment around growth stocks.

SCHD offers solid exposure to defensive sectors, which traditionally thrive when growth investors start getting nervous and reallocate their portfolios to increase defensive exposure. The weight of the technology sector in SCHD's portfolio is below 10%, which makes it a good play to protect portfolios against volatility in growth.

Another big advantage of SCHD is its heavy Energy exposure. Escalating Gulf instability pushes oil prices up, which is a huge tailwind for upstream companies like Chevron (CVX) and ConocoPhillips (COP). Brent crude price increased by (CO1:COM) increased by 38% YTD because energy facilities of various oil-rich Middle East countries are under threat due to the increasing instability in the region even though they are not directly involved in this military operation. And it is not only about upstream operations of Middle East countries but about one of the world's major oil export routes - the Strait of Hormuz, via which millions of barrels are transported every day. According to the Guardian, Iran has largely halted oil and gas exports through this vital route. Thus, increasing geopolitical uncertainty around this oil-rich region will highly likely push crude oil prices further. SCHD's 21% Energy exposure makes this ETF extremely well positioned to capitalize on growing oil prices.

Moreover, let's keep in mind that besides CVX and COP, there are a few other large beneficiaries of the current geopolitical situation. If we continue speaking about energy, SCHD also holds a stake in Coterra Energy (CTRA), a company with substantial natural gas exposure. We all know that the demand for natural gas is soaring due to energy-thirsty data centers. However, there is another tailwind for natural gas due to the Gulf's instability. The recent drone attack from Iran has led to a shutdown of Qatar Energy's large LNG facilities due to physical damage. As a result, LNG prices in Europe skyrocketed. Europe is a big LNG importer from the United States, and increasing LNG prices mean that the largest LNG producers of the U.S. will do their best to capitalize on the favorable pricing environment. Since LNG is produced from natural gas, it means price tailwinds for natural gas producers as well.

Finally, let's not forget that the largest individual holding of SCHD is Lockheed Martin Corporation (LMT), the major supplier of the U.S. Department of War. This company produces flagship F-35 military aircraft, which are vital for the U.S. military operation in Iran. Fresh reports suggest that President Trump's administration officials are set to meet executives of major defense contractors to boost output as stockpiles need to be replenished after the large military operation in Iran. This is another solid structural tailwind for one of SCHD's primary holdings.

Thus, we have a convergence of two massive uncertainty factors: the AI bubble narrative plus Gulf instability. In this environment, SCHD is poised to maintain its robust share price momentum for longer. Besides its solid defensive exposure, SCHD still offers by far the best mix of dividend yield and growth among its close peers. According to the below comparison, SCHD offers the highest yield of 3.32% and the highest historical dividend growth.

All the positive structural factors, together with SCHD's premium dividend scorecard, make me confident that this ETF will maintain its impressive share price momentum for longer. However, I have to admit that there are risks as well.

Reasons To Avoid SCHD

Since SCHD is a highly defensive ETF, its performance also depends on the sentiment around riskier assets. The greedier investors become, the more they are keen to invest in aggressively growth-oriented companies. There is an intensifying AI bubble narrative, which is difficult to argue. However, I, personally, do not think that there is a bubble. The largest tech companies with AI exposure drive revenue growth acceleration thanks to adding new AI-powered features to their product suites. Digital advertising companies like Google and Meta increase the quality of targeting using AI capabilities, which creates real value for their advertising customers.

The entire AI-related market is growing really fast, and CEO Jensen Huang said during Nvidia's earnings call that tokens are becoming more and more profitable. Therefore, the weakness of growth stocks' share price performance in 2026 is more sentiment-driven than fundamentals-driven. Since sentiment is something that is very difficult to forecast, investors might become unexpectedly extremely greedy again. If they see that the largest tech stocks resume their massive rallies, investors are likely to be rushing to rotate back from value to growth. In this case, SCHD might see a selloff that will erase its impressive YTD gains.

It is also important to keep in mind that over the long term, growth stocks tend to outperform defensive ones. The reason is simple: all these cloud-native capabilities and seamless integrations give tech companies an advantage to scale up faster compared to defensive companies, which need to build factories or pipelines to drive revenue growth. Therefore, over the long term, SCHD's relatively low exposure to the technology sector is a disadvantage.

The Verdict

I continue buying SCHD amid the strengthening AI bubble narrative converging with the increasing geopolitical uncertainty because various largest holdings of this ETF are well-positioned to capitalize on the current environment. SCHD's defensive exposure is extremely attractive in the current setup with growth investors becoming increasingly nervous. Finally, SCHD offers an unparalleled dividend scorecard among its closest rival ETFs. This defensive ETF remains a Strong Buy, in my opinion.

data.title: Invesco Short Term Bond Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Key takeaways

  1. Potential to lock in yields Bond yields, though down from their highs, are still at attractive levels compared to the last 10 years, in our view. Investors can lock in these yields for years to come by investing in high grade bonds before interest rates come down further.
  2. Picking your spots We believe numerous important factors enhance the attractiveness of investment grade credit. We observe that yield spreads between Treasuries and non-Treasury sectors have been tight, which makes our credit underwriting and security selection even more important.
  3. Beating the crowd Investors have amassed large amounts in cash and money markets (Source: St. Louis Fed. Data as of 12/31/25). As US Federal Reserve (Fed) rate cutting resumes and interest rates fall, cash will likely move to risk assets, pushing bond prices up and yields down. Timing a move is hard, but for now, investors are in our view being paid to wait in fixed income.

Investment objective

The fund seeks total return, comprised of current income and capital appreciation.

Fund facts

Fund AUM ($M) 2,093.19

Portfolio managers

Michael Hyman, Chuck Burge, Matthew Brill, Todd Schomberg

Manager perspective and outlook

  • Market sentiment during the quarter appeared mixed. The US unemployment rate rose and consumer spending growth appears to have slowed, both likely affected by the longest government shutdown in US history. Still, overall economic growth was buoyed by investment in artificial intelligence (AI) and related sectors. Corporate fundamentals remained firm following a strong earnings season and elevated capital market activity. However, a divergence of economic confidence between CEOs and consumers raised concerns about an economic divide between higher and lower income earners. AI “bubble” fears appeared to further dampen sentiment.
  • A weakening labor market led the Fed to resume rate cuts, reducing the federal funds rate twice, for a target range of 3.50-3.75%, the lowest since 2022. Markets expect two more rate cuts in 2026 as the Fed has expressed a desire to get interest rates close to a neutral stance. Credit spreads – the yield difference between Treasuries and US investment grade bonds – widened due to moderate volatility early in the quarter but have remained historically tight.
  • Our bond outlook is constructive. Market-implied recession probabilities in our view remain well below the expectations of surveyed economists, and interest rate stability, if it materializes, would likely further support demand. Our view is that growth will accelerate in 2026 but might face a headwind of elevated inflation early in the year.

We maintain a positive outlook for investment grade credit and maintain the fund's overweight in the sector. Based on the segment's health, we believe investment grade corporates remain attractive on a risk-adjusted basis. In our view, corporate fundamentals remain firm, the yield backdrop appears attractive and lower average dollar prices for bonds across the index present discounted buying opportunities, potentially enhancing downside protection for bondholders.

As the Fed's rate cutting has resumed, our perspective is that, in the current environment, the bond market benefits from rate cuts regardless of what drives them. If the Fed cuts because inflation has sustainably cooled, yield spreads between Treasuries and US investment grade bonds could remain tight or even compress further. Conversely, if cuts come in response to weaker growth, investment grade credit should still hold up – high-quality bonds generally become more valuable and income remains durable. Therefore, we have a constructive view of bonds in the intermediate part of the yield curve, which captures income and price appreciation potential while reducing excess duration risk.

Within the investment grade corporate segment, we maintain an overweight in financials, particularly in the banking sector. While valuations in the sector have narrowed toward long-term median levels, we believe the strength of corporate fundamentals still supports a positive outlook.

During the quarter, we decreased the fund's overweight in non-Agency mortgages and increased the fund's overweights in commercial mortgage-backed securities (CMBS) and asset-backed securities (ABS). Overweights in high-yield corporates and emerging markets were unchanged during the quarter. Yields and income are in our view attractive in those segments; however, we believe diligence is critical given potential for heightened volatility.

We kept the fund's duration fairly neutral compared to the benchmark, lengthening it due to potential slowing of US growth and as a hedge to credit risk.

data.title: Royal Caribbean Cruises: Buy On Dips
data.description:
Since I last wrote about Royal Caribbean Cruises Ltd. (RCL) in December, the stock is up by 8%. This was expected. What wasn't expected, however, was that its performance would surpass peers. Consider the chart below that traces the price performance of cruise stocks YTD. RCL is not just the best performing, but besides Viking Holdings (VIK), it's the only one to see an uptick so far in 2026, as Carnival Corporation (CCL) and Norwegian Cruise Line Holdings (NCLH) have seen a correction.

While there are good reasons for RCL's rise, starting with its results for 2025 and outlook for 2026, investors should brace for pullbacks too, even as the stock's medium-to-long-term story still looks good. All of this is discussed below.

Why Has RCL Outperformed?

A closer look at RCL's price chart reveals that the real uptick followed the release of its full-year 2025 results. On the day, the stock was up by no less than 19%, with key takeaways from the report as below:

  • Revenues surpass trend growth: The company's revenue growth came in at 8.8%, which is a significant slowing down from last year when it grew by 18.6%. This represents a slowing down even from pre-pandemic levels, as revenues rose by 15.3% even in 2019. I wouldn't be too concerned as yet, though, since growth is still higher than trend levels, with the CAGR for the past 10 years at 8%.
  • Net yield growth fares better than forecast: Net yields, defined as the adjusted gross margin per average passenger cruise day (APCD), grew by 3.7%, notably higher than the guidance range of 2.2-2.7%.
  • Adjusted EPS exceeds guidance: The company also marginally exceeded the profit guidance. While the company had a guidance range of USD 15.58-15.63 for adjusted EPS, the actual number came in at USD 15.64.
  • Guidance reflects further earnings growth: The company's guidance added to some of the strength of the results, with a 15% growth in adjusted EPS in 2026. Even though this is a significant slowing down from the 32.5% increase in 2025, on its own, it looks robust.

Market Multiples Aren't Bad

Supporting the company's fundamentals are the market multiples. Based on the guidance for 2026, the forward non-GAAP P/E is at 16x, which is below the stock's five-year average of 17.1x, indicating just small upside this year. It's marginally higher than the average of 15x for cruise stocks, including RCL. This is the same scenario as when I checked the last time, but right now it bears more weight for two reasons:

  • At the time, RCL had faced a significant pullback, losing some 25% of its value between August and November 2025. As a fundamentally strong cruise stock and the only one paying dividends. However, now CCL too has started paying a dividend and, in fact, has a marginally bigger forward yield at 2.1% compared to RCL at 2%.
  • Further, back in December, the broader environment suggested a need for caution, as it does now (discussed more below), which in turn can make investors shy of travel stocks.

Still, over the medium to long term, RCL still looks good. Analysts' estimates on Seeking Alpha show that its forward P/E for 2028 is down to just 12.1x, and by 2031, it touches 8.8x, both much lower than the stock's five-year average P/E. This indicates that the stock has the potential to rise by over 40% over the next three years and can double in the next six.

Rising Risks From Geopolitics

The happy earnings and price outlook is at risk from the current geopolitical climate, however. Even though the Middle East is a minuscule proportion of the company's revenues, the stresses in the region can still impact Royal Caribbean negatively in the following ways:

  • Rising fuel expenses:

The price of Brent crude is up by 24.6% over the past month. Updated forecasts indicate the likelihood of further increase in Q2 2026. While the company had already penciled in a 2.4% increase in fuel expenses in 2026, based on actual prices at the time, increased crude prices could lead to an upward revision in these projections.

Still, if peace returns soon enough, the rise in fuel costs, which accounted for only 12.6% of the total in 2025, could be manageable. In case of an oil price shock, though, they could have an outsized impact on total costs.

  • Second round impact of higher oil prices:

Let's stay a moment with the implications of a potential oil price shock, which isn't a far-fetched idea either. Sustained oil prices at over USD 100/bbl will have second-round impacts through their effect on the price of other goods and services' costs as well, leading to more broad-based cost increases. Also, inflation as such can be higher, which in turn can induce a consumer slowdown, with consumer discretionary stocks like cruises the first to be impacted.

  • Unchanged debt levels over three years:

The situation can be complicated for Royal Caribbean considering its debt levels. The company's net debt almost doubled between 2019 and 2022 on account of the pandemic and has dropped by just 4% since.

For now, there's a lot of comfort to be gained from the net debt-to-equity ratios over time. From 1.1x in 2019, the number ballooned to 7.6x in 2022, but with improvement in the equity value, it's now down to 1.7x. If the equity value keeps improving, the ratio could be back to 2019 levels in a relatively short period of time. But if a slowdown were to indeed happen, the debt could look far less sustainable.

What Next?

To sum up, the situation is as follows. Fundamentally, there's still a case for RCL. Its recent earnings are strong, the outlook is healthy, and even near-term market multiples indicate small upside. Dividends add to its attractiveness. Especially for medium- to long-term investors, RCL can unlock much value considering robust earnings estimates and attractive forward P/E.

But right now, there's a big risk on the horizon too. The geopolitical situation and rising oil prices have created uncertainty at a time when the company's debt levels have remained relatively unchanged over the past three years.

While the situation is forecast to stabilize, I'd expect an increase in RCL's price in the near term. But unless the situation goes completely out of hand, there can still be significant upside to the stock over the medium-to-long term. So I'm retaining a Buy rating with that perspective, treating any short-term dips as opportunities to buy.

data.title: 3 Major Questions For Investors In March
data.description:
Equity markets have held up much better than expected this week given the opening up of a significant regional conflict in Iran, which has caused energy prices to spike. Well, at least U.S. equities have weathered the storm well. Asia stock markets have been hit with considerably more volatility in this week's trading sessions.

The conflict in Iran is just the latest key worry for investors that has emerged in recent months. U.S. equities have remained quite resilient in recent weeks despite volatility rising and with the market trading at historically high valuations. However, these events have spurred major new questions for investors. And how these three key issues get resolved in the coming weeks and months could determine the direction of the markets.

#1 - What Is The End Game In Iran?

This is the question that will likely dominate the headlines in the coming days and weeks. To me, regime change in Iran seems a very unlikely outcome, outside a huge number of 'boots on the ground', something the administration has stated it would avoid. No significant regime change in history has been accomplished via air power alone.

A civil war like those recently in Libya or Syria is always a possibility over the longer term. But little appears to be happening on the ground in Iran to point to that scenario. If a civil war took place in Iran, there could be a migrant inflow into Europe that would make the Syrian migration wave of the mid-2010s look like a walk in the park. Iran has some 90 million people, and the country is roughly half the size of Europe on a land mass basis.

While using $4 million Patriot missiles to shoot down $25,000 drones and $250,000 to $500,000 ballistic missiles is a great business model for Raytheon (RTX), it is not a cost-effective way to fight a war. More importantly, it is not sustainable given the limited number of interceptors in the U.S. arsenal and needs in other regions (Ukraine).

Therefore, the most likely scenario in my view is some sort of negotiated end to this conflict will happen several weeks down the line, perhaps along the lines of what recently happened in Venezuela. With enough concessions to allow both sides to save face after Iranian military assets have been degraded further and key munitions start to run out on the U.S./Israel side.

However, events on the ground could always change that calculus. In the meantime, markets will continue to be hit with volatility from news on the ground. The longer this conflict drags on the more upward pressure it will put on oil prices. This will be a significant tailwind for inflation, which in turn reduces the chances of further rate cuts from the Federal Reserve until resolved.

The impacts to the rest of the world will be higher from Operation Epic Fury, which has curtailed most of the crude exports out of the Middle East as traffic has largely ceased flowing through the Strait of Hormuz, where some 20% of global crude shipments go through. This region is particularly a huge source of energy supplies to Asia. Roughly 80% (1.4 million bpd) of Iranian crude exports go to China it should be noted. A key reason that stock exchanges in this region like the Kospi have been so volatile over the past week.

Interestingly, Kharg Island which is located off the country's southern coast in the northern Persian Gulf, has not been hit in this conflict. 80% to 90% of Iranian oil exports flow out of this massive supertanker loading facility. Taking Kharg Island offline would cripple the Iranian regime financially. It would also trigger a huge spike in oil prices and would also likely result in retaliatory strikes on energy facilities in Saudi Arabia and other Gulf states.

#2 - How Many More 'Cockroaches' Will Emerge In The Credit Markets?'

Before news from Iran overtook headlines, the deterioration in parts of the credit markets were one of the two main worries for investors in February. Events at Blue Owl Capital (OWL) triggered new worries around potential new 'cockroaches' in the burgeoning private credit markets. These worries had largely remained dormant since the immediate aftermath of the unexpected bankruptcies of First Brands and Tricolor Holdings late last summer.

Last week, a UK mortgage provider called Market Financial Solutions collapsed. This week, Blackstone (BX) announced it had record redemption requests (7.9% of AUM) in its $82 billion private credit fund. Jamie Dimon recently warned that the leader of JPMorgan Chase & Co. (JPM) sees some similarities to the lead up to the Great Financial Crisis present in today's credit markets. Other prominent financial leaders like Lloyd Blankfein have been issuing warnings on the private credit markets recently as well. So, will significant new problems emerge from credit markets in the weeks and months ahead? Prudent investors should be monitoring these developments closely.

#3 - Are Fears Of AI Disruption Overblown?

The other big issue in February was growing fears around potential AI disruption. The now infamous 'SaaSpocalypse' opened the month and pressured many parts of the software sector throughout February. These fears also hit other industries sporadically. The stocks of insurance brokers, commercial real estate services, wealth management concerns, and logistical/trucking firms all took their turn in the barrel during the month.

Then late last week, Block (XYZ) disclosed it was laying off approximately 4,000 employees, roughly 40% of its entire workforce. The company's CEO stated this action was taken due to improving AI capabilities that made these functions redundant. This ignited new fears that AI will disrupt the U.S. jobs markets faster than previously forecasted and XYZ was rewarded by a 17% rally in its stock following the reduction in force notice. Wednesday's better than expected February ADP jobs report was a relief, although job openings remain at a five-year low.

Will March bring new layoff announcements akin to Block's and new fears AI will upend entire industries? Definitely something investors should monitor. The flip side to this question, is if AI doesn't prove to be massively disruptive, how will hyperscalers and firms like OpenAI (OPENAI) earn an acceptable return on their massive AI investments?

I continue to be cautious on the overall market. Equities feel like they are priced close to perfection within a market backdrop that is far less than ideal. Therefore, my model portfolio continues to consist of roughly a 75% allocation to covered call holdings around the few stocks in this market that sport reasonable valuations. Most of the rest of my portfolio is in short-term treasuries and cash, awaiting lower entry points.

data.title: Touchstone Small Company Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Fund Highlights

  • Invests primarily in common stocks of small companies
  • Research intensive approach focuses on finding high quality companies that are believed to have improving business models, solid management teams, sustainable growth potential and favorable industry dynamics
  • Quality companies are those that possess the following traits: consistent earnings, reasonable valuations, low debt levels, solid free cash flows and strong management with a history of good capital allocation

Market Recap

Volatility during the quarter was significantly lower than the first three quarters of the year, which witnessed the Russell 2000 Index experience a bear market decline of 22% from the start of the year to the April 8 lows. Then, the Russell 2000 rallied 50% to the mid-December high. We believe the small cap rally was driven by relief that tariff rates were lowered and did not impact the economy and corporate earnings as originally feared; the “One Big Beautiful Bill” provided fiscal stimulus and removed policy uncertainty, and the U.S. Federal Reserve provided monetary stimulus through lower interest rate targets and expectations. All of this provided a more favorable backdrop for small cap earnings.

The benchmark sectors that outperformed during the quarter were Health Care, Communication Services, Materials, and Real Estate. The benchmark sectors that underperformed for the quarter were Consumer Staples, Information Technology (IT), Consumer Discretionary, Industrials, Utilities, Financials, and Energy.

Portfolio Review

The Touchstone Small Company Fund (Class A Shares, Load Waived)(SAGWX) outperformed its benchmark, the Russell 2000 Index, for the quarter ended December 31, 2025.

The Fund’s top three performing sectors in terms of adding value were IT and Industrials, both driven by stock selection, and Consumer Staples, due to an underweight allocation. The Fund’s bottom three performing sectors that detracted from relative performance were Health Care, Consumer Discretionary, and Materials, due primarily to weak stock performance.

Performance was driven by strong gains across Health Care and IT holdings. Haemonetics Corp. (HAE)(Health Care sector) rebounded sharply following better-than-expected growth in its plasma business, validating our decision to add on prior weakness given durable growth catalysts. Tower Semiconductor Ltd. (TSEM) and Ciena Corp. (CIEN)(both IT sector) also posted outsized returns as accelerating artificial intelligence infrastructure investment fueled demand for silicon photonics and optical networking equipment. Additional contributions came from Globus Medical Inc. (GMED)(Health Care sector), where operational improvements led to stronger earnings growth, and Progyny Inc. (PGNY)(Health Care sector), which benefited from improving utilization trends and strong client additions that enhanced long-term growth visibility.

Detractors were largely driven by company-specific execution issues and valuation resets. Stride Inc. (LRN)(Consumer Discretionary sector) declined following disruptions tied to a software platform transition that impacted enrollment growth, though the position is being closely monitored. Doximity Inc. (DOCS) and Certara Inc. (CERT)(both Health Care sector) sold off amid investor concerns over biopharma marketing and spending visibility in 2026, prompting us to add at more attractive valuations given intact fundamentals. Commvault Systems Inc. (CVLT)(IT sector) declined as reported decelerating subscription growth led to valuation compression despite continued strength in underlying recurring revenue. Grand Canyon Education Inc. (LOPE)(Consumer Discretionary sector) also declined amid broader weakness in education services stocks, despite reporting strong results, and was added on weakness.

During the quarter, the Fund initiated positions in Franklin Electric Co. Inc. (FELE) (Industrials sector), LCI Industries Inc. (LCII)(Consumer Discretionary sector), Q2 Holdings Inc. (QTWO)(IT sector), Tidewater Inc. (TDW)(Energy sector), Waystar Holding Corp. (WAY)(Health Care sector), and Zeta Global Holdings Corp. (ZETA)(IT sector), reflecting opportunities across various sectors. The portfolio exited CSG Systems International Inc. (CSGS)(IT sector) following the announcement of its acquisition.

Outlook and Conclusion

Small cap and large cap 2026 earnings are both expected to increase 15%. Small cap relative valuations to large cap hit a 25-year low in mid-November, and while modestly off the low remain at a 30% discount. We have been writing for three years the small cap vs. large cap performance was driven primarily by fundamentals, i.e., significantly better relative large cap earnings growth. With earnings growth now on par and with discounted valuation, the small cap risk/reward is the most favorable it has been in several years.

Small cap price returns in 2025 were driven by non-earners (+31%); low quality (lowest quintile ROE +25%); and high beta (top quintile +20%). This happens in market environments like we experienced from the April lows. We believe that the fourth quarter market environment could reflect a return to fundamentals driving equity returns. In our view, that type of market environment is favorable to the Fund, which invests only in profitable small cap stocks with above average quality and below average volatility. We continue to execute our investment process consistently, identify compelling new investments, manage risk, and target the consistent risk/return profile the Fund has delivered since inception.
